questions.text: A motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies is called a(n) _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A suggests that a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies are called a motor unit. This statement is correct. A motor unit consists of a motor neuron (nerve cell) and all the muscle fibers (muscle cells) it innervates or supplies. When the motor neuron is stimulated, all the muscle fibers in the motor unit contract simultaneously.

Option B suggests that a neuromuscular junction is the term for a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies. This statement is not correct. The neuromuscular junction is a specialized synapse between a motor neuron and a muscle fiber. It is the point where the motor neuron releases neurotransmitters (acetylcholine) to stimulate the muscle fiber to contract. It is a crucial structure for communication between the nervous system and muscles, but it does not encompass all the muscle fibers supplied by a single motor neuron.

Option C suggests that a neural unit is the term for a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies. This statement is not correct. The term "neural unit" is not a standard or widely used in biology or neuroscience.

Option D suggests that microtubules are the term for a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies. This statement is not correct. Microtubules are a component of the cytoskeleton of cells and are involved in various cellular processes, but they are not related to the concept of a motor neuron and the muscle fibers it innervates.

questions.explanation: A motor unit consists of a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it supplies, so the correct answer is Option A. The neuromuscular junction is a synapse between the terminal motor neuron and the muscle fiber.

questions.text: Muscles are composed of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Option A suggests that muscles are composed of silica. This statement is not correct. Silica is a chemical compound of silicon and oxygen, and it is not a major component of muscles. Muscles primarily consist of proteins, water, minerals, and other organic compounds, but not silica.

Option B suggests that muscles are composed of polyester threads. This statement is not correct. Polyester is a synthetic polymer and has no relation to the composition of muscles. Muscles are made up of specialized muscle fibers, connective tissues, blood vessels, and nerves.

Option C suggests that muscles are composed of groups of cell fibers. This statement is partly correct. Muscles are indeed composed of groups of muscle fibers. Muscle fibers are long, cylindrical cells that contain contractile proteins, actin, and myosin, responsible for muscle contraction. Multiple muscle fibers are bundled together to form muscle tissue, and different muscle tissues combine to make muscles.

Option D suggests that muscles are composed of calcium and phosphorus. This statement is not correct. While calcium and phosphorus are essential minerals for various biological processes in the body, they are not the primary components of muscles. As mentioned earlier, muscles are mainly composed of proteins (actin and myosin), water, and other organic compounds.

questions.explanation: Option (c) suggests that muscles are composed of groups of cell fibers. This statement is partly correct. Muscles are indeed composed of groups of muscle fibers. Muscle fibers are long, cylindrical cells that contain contractile proteins, actin, and myosin, responsible for muscle contraction. Multiple muscle fibers are bundled together to form muscle tissues, and different muscle tissues combine to make muscles.

questions.text: The fibrous connective tissue that attaches a muscle to a bone is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones. They are strong and flexible, allowing them to transmit the force generated by muscles to the bones, enabling movement at the joints. Tendons play a crucial role in the musculoskeletal system by providing stability and facilitating movement.

Ligaments are also fibrous connective tissues, but they serve a different function. Ligaments connect bones to other bones at the joints and provide stability to the joints, preventing excessive movement and protecting them from injury.

Reticular tissue is a type of connective tissue found in various organs and tissues, such as the liver, spleen, and lymph nodes. It forms a supportive framework within these organs but does not attach muscles to bones.

Cartilage is a type of connective tissue that is softer and more flexible than bone. It is found in various parts of the body, such as the nose, ears, and joints. Cartilage does not attach muscles to bones; instead, it provides cushioning and support at the joints.

questions.explanation: Option (a) is correct. Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that attach muscles to bones. They are strong and flexible, allowing them to transmit the force generated by muscles to the bones, enabling movement at the joints. questions.text: A cell without a cell wall is termed as?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tonoplast is a specialized membrane found in plant cells, particularly in the vacuole. It is responsible for maintaining the osmotic balance within the cell by controlling the movement of ions and other substances in and out of the vacuole.

A protoplast is a cell that lacks a cell wall. It usually refers to a plant cell from which the cell wall has been removed through enzymatic treatment. Protoplasts are useful in various biotechnological applications, such as genetic engineering and somatic hybridization, as they can be fused together to create new hybrid cells.

The symplast refers to the continuous interconnected network of cytoplasm present in plant cells. It allows for the movement of substances, such as ions and water, between neighboring cells through plasmodesmata. While it is related to plant cells, it is not the term used for a cell without a cell wall.

Epiblast refers to a specific layer of cells in the early embryo during the process of embryogenesis. It is essential for the formation of the three primary germ layers, from which all the tissues and organs of the organism develop. It is not the term used for a cell without a cell wall.

questions.explanation: A vacuole membrane is called the tonoplast. The symplast pathway is the movement of water molecules between cytoplasm, plasma membrane, and plasmodesmata. Epiblast is the outermost layer of an embryo. Hence the answer is protoplast, Option B.

questions.text: _ is responsible for making ribosomal RNA (rRNA).
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: a) Nucleus only: This option is not correct. While the nucleus plays a central role in the control of cellular activities and contains the genetic material (DNA), it is not directly responsible for making ribosomal RNA (rRNA). However, the nucleus contains the genes that code for rRNA, and the synthesis of rRNA takes place within a specialized region of the nucleus called the nucleolus.

b) Nucleolus only: This option is correct. The nucleolus is a distinct structure located within the nucleus of eukaryotic cells. It is primarily responsible for making ribosomal RNA (rRNA) and assembling ribosomes. The nucleolus contains the genes for rRNA, and it is involved in the transcription and processing of rRNA molecules, which are essential components of the ribosomes.

c) Nucleus and nucleolus only: This option is not correct because the nucleus itself is not directly responsible for making rRNA. As mentioned earlier, the nucleolus, which is a part of the nucleus, is responsible for rRNA synthesis.
d) None of the above: This option is not correct as well because option (b) is the correct one. The nucleolus is responsible for making ribosomal RNA (rRNA) in eukaryotic cells.

questions.explanation: Molecules of rRNA are synthesized in a specialized region of the cell "nucleus" called the "nucleolus", which appears as a dense area within the "nucleus" and contains the genes that encode rRNA.

This statement is also not correct. Flagella are whip-like appendages used for cell movement, and they are found in both prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells. However, the structure and function of flagella in prokaryotes and eukaryotes are different.

questions.text: Anti-venom given after a snake bite venom is an example of:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Artificial active immunity refers to the development of immunity through the administration of a vaccine or antigen. In this case, the anti-venom given after a snakebite is not an example of artificial active immunity, as it does not involve the administration of a vaccine.

Artificial passive immunity occurs when preformed antibodies are directly administered to an individual. In the case of a snakebite, anti-venom is administered to neutralize the effects of the snake venom. The antibodies in the anti-venom provide immediate protection and help to neutralize the venom toxins.

Natural active immunity refers to the development of immunity through natural exposure to an infectious agent. It occurs when the body's immune system recognizes and responds to a pathogen, leading to the production of specific antibodies. It is not applicable in the context of receiving anti-venom after a snakebite.

Natural passive immunity occurs when antibodies are transferred from a mother to her offspring, typically through breastfeeding or placental transfer. It provides temporary protection to the newborn until their own immune system develops. It is not relevant to the administration of anti-venom after a snakebite.

questions.explanation: Therefore, the correct answer is B) Artificial passive immunity, as the administration of anti-venom provides immediate protection through the transfer of preformed antibodies. Artificially acquired passive immunity is protection acquired by giving a person an injection or transfusion of antibodies made by someone else. These antibodies neutralize the infectious agents in the usual way, but the protection lasts only a few weeks because the antibodies gradually break down and are not replaced.

questions.text: The capillaries of glomerulus rejoin to form an _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The glomerular capillaries reunite and form the efferent arteriole which exits at the vascular pole. The efferent arterioles of cortical nephrons give rise to the peritubular capillaries.

Afferent arterioles have two functions: they serve to connect the renal artery to the glomerular capillaries and they take an active role in controlling not only the local blood pressure inside the kidney but also your overall body's blood pressure.

Peritubular capillaries are tiny blood vessels in your kidneys. They filter waste from your blood so the waste can leave your body through urine. Peritubular capillaries also reabsorb nutrients your body needs to work properly, such as minerals.

questions.text: Blood solute potential is controlled by the following hormone.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Estrogen is a primary female sex hormone that regulates the development and maintenance of the female reproductive system, as well as secondary sexual characteristics such as breast development and pubic hair growth.

Epinephrine, also known as adrenaline, is a hormone secreted by the adrenal medulla. It is released during strong emotions such as fear or anger and increases heart rate, blood pressure, muscle strength, and blood glucose levels to prepare the body for “fight-or-flight” responses.

Thyroxine (T4) is the principal hormone secreted by the thyroid gland, and it plays essential roles in regulating metabolism, digestion, heart and muscle function, brain development, and bone maintenance.

Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also called vasopressin, is produced by the hypothalamus and stored in the posterior pituitary. It regulates the body’s water balance by signaling the kidneys to reabsorb water, thereby controlling blood volume, osmolarity, and solute concentration.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct.Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), also called vasopressin, is produced by the hypothalamus and released by the posterior pituitary. It helps the kidneys reabsorb water, reducing urine volume and making it more concentrated. By conserving water, ADH regulates blood volume, blood pressure, and osmolarity. questions.explanation: Transduction is a process in which genetic material is transferred between bacterial cells through the action of bacteriophages (viruses that infect bacteria). This transferred DNA can recombine with the recipient cell's genome, leading to genetic variation and recombination.

questions.text: The number of spermatid produced from primary spermatocyte is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is partly right at first glance. After meiosis I, a primary spermatocyte divides into two secondary spermatocytes, and each of those cells is haploid. However, these secondary spermatocytes are not spermatids yet. They still undergo meiosis II, a second division that produces spermatids. Each of the two secondary spermatocytes produces two spermatids, resulting in a total of four spermatids from one primary spermatocyte. Therefore, the number “2” only represents an intermediate stage (secondary spermatocytes), not the final number of spermatids.

This option is also incorrect, although it may seem partly right at first glance. After meiosis I, a primary spermatocyte divides into two secondary spermatocytes, and each of those cells is haploid. However, these secondary spermatocytes are not spermatids yet. They still undergo meiosis II, a second division that produces spermatids. Each of the two secondary spermatocytes produces two spermatids, resulting in a total of four spermatids from one primary spermatocyte. Therefore, the number “2” only represents an intermediate stage (secondary spermatocytes), not the final number of spermatids.

This option is incorrect and biologically impossible because cell division through meiosis always produces numbers in powers of two (2, 4, 8, etc.), not odd numbers. The process of spermatogenesis is a highly regulated and symmetrical type of cell division. One diploid primary spermatocyte divides evenly into two haploid secondary spermatocytes (after meiosis I), and each of those divides evenly again to produce two haploid spermatids (after meiosis II). Therefore, there can never be three spermatids from one primary spermatocyte.

This option is correct. During spermatogenesis, a primary spermatocyte (2n) undergoes two meiotic divisions. The first division (meiosis I) results in two secondary spermatocytes (n), each containing half the number of chromosomes. Then, during meiosis II, each of these secondary spermatocytes divides again to form two spermatids, leading to a total of four haploid (n) spermatids. These spermatids are immature and non-motile cells that will later undergo a process called spermiogenesis, where they mature into functional, motile sperm cells (spermatozoa). This ensures that each sperm cell contains half the genetic information necessary to combine with an ovum during fertilization. Hence, from one primary spermatocyte, four spermatids are produced.

questions.explanation: During spermatogenesis, which is the process of sperm cell development, a primary spermatocyte undergoes two rounds of cell division to produce four haploid cells called spermatids. This process ensures that the resulting sperm cells (spermatozoa) have half the number of chromosomes as the original cell. The spermatids then undergo further maturation to become mature sperm cells.

questions.text: The mechanism of enzyme activation is referred to as?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Activation energy refers to the minimum amount of energy that reactants must possess to undergo a chemical reaction. It is the energy barrier that must be overcome for a reaction to occur. Enzymes lower the activation energy required for a chemical reaction, making it easier for the reaction to proceed.

Catalysis refers to the process in which a catalyst (such as an enzyme) facilitates or speeds up a chemical reaction without being consumed or permanently altered during the reaction. Enzymes act as biological catalysts, accelerating specific chemical reactions in living organisms.

Enzyme specificity refers to the ability of an enzyme to bind only to specific substrates and catalyze specific chemical reactions. Each enzyme has a unique active site that matches the shape and chemical properties of its substrate, allowing it to bind with high specificity.

Denaturation is the process by which an enzyme loses its shape and, as a result, its function due to changes in temperature, pH, or other environmental factors. When an enzyme denatures, it loses its ability to bind to its substrate and catalyze reactions effectively.

questions.explanation: The mechanism of enzyme activation is referred to as "enzyme catalysis" or simply "catalysis." Enzymes are biological catalysts that facilitate chemical reactions by lowering the activation energy required for those reactions to occur. This allows reactions to proceed at a faster rate or under milder conditions than they would without the enzyme. Enzymes achieve this by providing an active site where reactants (substrates) can bind and undergo chemical transformations, making the reaction more efficient.

questions.text: Covalent modification means change in?

options.explanation: Covalent modification refers to the chemical alteration of an enzyme through the addition or removal of specific chemical groups (like phosphate, methyl, or acetyl groups) to certain amino acid residues. These changes are covalent in nature, meaning they form strong, permanent bonds, unlike reversible non-covalent interactions. Such modifications can significantly change the enzyme’s chemical properties, including its activity, stability, or ability to bind to substrates. For example, phosphorylation of enzymes often activates or deactivates them, thus regulating metabolic pathways.

This option is incorrect because covalent modifications primarily affect the chemical nature of enzymes rather than their physical state. Physical properties include aspects like solubility, density, or color, which are not directly altered by covalent attachment of groups. While the enzyme’s overall conformation may change, this is a consequence of altered chemical properties rather than direct modification of physical properties.

This option is incorrect. Covalent modification in the context of enzymes refers to a regulatory mechanism where the activity of an enzyme is altered through the covalent attachment or removal of chemical groups to specific amino acid residues within the enzyme molecule. This post-translational modification can result in changes to the enzyme's structure, conformation, and ultimately, its catalytic activity.

This option is incorrect. questions.text: The number of models that represent the enzyme-substrate model are?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Some students may think that there is only one model to explain enzyme–substrate interaction. This idea usually comes from learning only the lock-and-key concept in earlier studies. However, this is incorrect because research has shown that more than one model exists to explain how enzymes and substrates interact.

The correct answer is two models. These are the Lock-and-Key Model and the Induced Fit Model. The lock-and-key model, proposed by Emil Fischer in 1894, describes the active site as rigid and specifically shaped to match the substrate. Later, in 1959, Daniel Koshland proposed the induced fit model, which explained that enzymes are flexible, and their active site changes shape slightly to perfectly fit the substrate.

Suggesting three models is incorrect. Some learners may confuse other enzyme-related concepts, like the transition state theory or enzyme inhibition mechanisms, with models of enzyme-substrate interaction. However, these are not considered separate models of enzyme binding, so three is not the correct number.

This option is also wrong because no biological or biochemical reference supports the existence of four different enzyme–substrate models. Standard textbooks and scientific explanations only describe two well-accepted models, not four.

questions.explanation: There are two main models that represent the enzyme-substrate interaction:Lock-and-Key ModelInduced Fit Model

questions.text: The process through which animals take oxygen (O2) from the air and release carbon dioxide (CO2) into the air is called:

questions.text: A pea plant with yellow seeds was crossed to a plant having green seeds. What will happen in the F1 generation; if the plants are true breeding?

options.text: Half of the seeds will be yellow
options.explanation: suggests that half of the seeds in the F1 generation will be yellow. This statement is not correct. In genetics, the color of the seeds is determined by the expression of specific alleles at the seed color gene locus. If one parent has yellow seeds and the other has green seeds, and both parents are true-breeding (homozygous for their respective alleles), then all the F1 generation seeds will have the same genotype, which will be heterozygous for the seed color gene. In this case, the dominant yellow allele (Y) will mask the recessive green allele (y), resulting in all the seeds in the F1 generation being yellow.

options.explanation: suggests that all of the seeds in the F1 generation will be green. This statement is not correct. As mentioned above, the dominant yellow allele (Y) will mask the recessive green allele (y) in the F1 generation, resulting in all the seeds being yellow.

options.explanation: Suggests that both phenotypes (yellow and green seeds) will be present in a ratio of 1:2:1 in the F1 generation. This statement is not correct. The correct phenotypic ratio in the F1 generation of a cross between a true-breeding yellow-seeded plant (YY) and a true-breeding green-seeded plant (yy) is 100% yellow seeds (Yy).

options.text: All the seeds will be yellow
options.explanation: suggests that all the seeds in the F1 generation will be yellow. This statement is correct. Since the yellow seed color is dominant (Y) over the green seed color (y), all the seeds in the F1 generation will have the dominant allele (Y) and, therefore, be yellow.

questions.text: A certain type of plant is only tall when it has a heterozygous genotype. If two heterozygous plants are crossed, what is the probability their offspring will also be tall?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: If a certain type of plant is only tall when it has a heterozygous genotype, it suggests that the tall trait is dominant and the short trait is recessive. Let's use "T" to represent the dominant allele for tallness and "t" to represent the recessive allele for shortness. When two heterozygous plants (Tt x Tt) are crossed, each parent contributes one allele for tallness (T) and one allele for shortness (t). The possible genotypes of the offspring are: TT (tall), Tt (tall), Tt (tall, tt (short) Out of these four possible genotypes, three of them result in the tall phenotype (TT, Tt, Tt). Therefore, the probability that the offspring will be tall is 3 out of 4 possible outcomes. However, since this plant can only be tall if heterozygous, then 2/4 i.e., 50% of the offsprings will be tall.

Therefore, the probability that the offspring will be tall is 3 out of 4 possible outcomes. However, since this plant can only be tall if heterozygous, then 2/4 i.e., 50% of the offsprings will be tall.

questions.text: Which of the following statement about Avogadro's hypothesis is correct?
questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.topic: Introduction to Fundamental Concepts of Chemistry

options.text: Under similar conditions of temperature and pressure, gases react with each other in simple ratio.
options.explanation: Gases do not necessarily react with each other in simple ratios. While Avogadro's hypothesis states that equal volumes of gases contain the same number of particles, this does not mean that gases will react with each other in simple ratios. The ratio in which gases react depends on the specific chemical reaction taking place, as well as the temperature, pressure, and other conditions. In some cases, gases may react in simple ratios, while in other cases, the ratios may be more complex.

options.text: Under similar conditions of temperature and pressure, equal volumes of all gases contain same number of molecules.
options.explanation: Avogadro's hypothesis states that equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain the same number of particles, regardless of their chemical nature and physical properties. This hypothesis is the basis for the concept of the mole, which is a unit of measurement used in chemistry to express the amount of a substance. One mole of a substance is defined as the amount of that substance that contains the same number of particles as there are atoms in exactly 12 grams of carbon-12.

options.text: At NTP all gases contain same number of molecules.
options.explanation: Gases do not necessarily contain the same number of molecules at NTP (normal temperature and pressure). While gases at NTP have a standard temperature of 273.15 K (0 °C) and a standard pressure of 1 atmosphere (101.325 kPa), the number of molecules in a gas sample depends on the volume of the sample, the temperature, the pressure, and the number of moles of gas present. According to Avogadro's hypothesis, equal volumes of gases at the same temperature and pressure contain the same number of particles, but this does not necessarily mean that all gases contain the same number of molecules at NTP. The number of molecules in a gas sample can be calculated using the ideal gas law, which takes into account the volume, temperature, pressure, and number of moles of gas present.

options.text: Gases always react with gases only at given temperature and pressure.
options.explanation: Gases can react with other types of substances besides gases, such as liquids or solids. The ability of gases to react with other substances depends on the specific chemical reaction taking place, as well as the temperature, pressure, and other conditions. While gases are more likely to react with other gases under certain conditions, such as at high temperatures and pressures, this is not always the case. For example, many chemical reactions involving gases take place in the presence of a catalyst, which can help to facilitate the reaction between the gas and another substance.

questions.text: The transfer of an electron from an atom of _ ionization energy to another atom with _ electron affinity was called an ionic bond by Lewis.

Lewis, in describing ionic bonding, highlighted the transfer of electrons between two atoms with contrasting ionization energies and electron affinities. Specifically: An atom with low ionization energy tends to lose electrons easily, forming a positively charged ion (cation). An atom with high electron affinity tends to gain electrons readily, forming a negatively charged ion (anion). The transfer of an electron from the atom with low ionization energy to the atom with high electron affinity results in the formation of oppositely charged ions. The electrostatic attraction between these ions forms what is known as an ionic bond. This type of bonding is characteristic of ionic compounds, where ions of opposite charge attract each other to create a stable compound.

questions.text: Manufacturing of Ammonia by the Haber's process is an?
questions.subject: Chemistry

options.explanation: Endothermic reaction: An endothermic reaction is a reaction in which heat is absorbed. This means that the energy of the products is less than the energy of the reactants. The Haber process is not an endothermic reaction because it releases heat when nitrogen gas and hydrogen gas react to form ammonia.

The Haber process is an exothermic reaction because it releases heat when nitrogen gas and hydrogen gas react to form ammonia. The heat released by the Haber process is used to heat the reaction mixture and to drive the reaction forward. The enthalpy change for the Haber's Process is -92.46 kJ. The negative sign signifies it is an "exothermic reaction".

Irreversible reaction: An irreversible reaction is a reaction that cannot be reversed. This means that the products of the reaction cannot be converted back into the reactants. The Haber process is not an irreversible reaction because the ammonia that is produced can be converted back into nitrogen gas and hydrogen gas.
Slow reaction: A slow reaction is a reaction that takes a long time to complete. The Haber process is not a slow reaction because it is typically completed in a few minutes.

questions.explanation: The manufacturing of ammonia by the Haber's process is an exothermic reaction. The Haber's process is a well-known industrial method for the synthesis of ammonia (NH3) from nitrogen (N2) and hydrogen (H2) gases. The balanced chemical equation for the reaction is: N2 + 3H2 ⇌ 2NH3 In this reaction, nitrogen and hydrogen gases react to produce ammonia gas. The forward reaction (from left to right) is exothermic, meaning it releases heat to the surroundings. As a result, the reaction is favored at lower temperatures. The Haber's process is carried out at high pressure and moderate temperature to achieve a good yield of ammonia while considering both the reaction rate and the equilibrium position. By removing the heat generated in the exothermic reaction, the process can be made more efficient and productive.

questions.text: The waves in which the particles of the medium vibrate about their mean position along the direction of propagation of the waves are called
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Longitudinal waves are waves in which the particles of the medium vibrate back and forth along the direction of propagation of the wave. This means that the particles compress and expand in the same direction as the wave is traveling. Examples of longitudinal waves include sound waves in a gas or liquid, and seismic P-waves in the Earth.
In transverse waves, the particles of the medium vibrate perpendicular to the direction of propagation of the wave. Examples include light waves, radio waves, and seismic S-waves.

Mechanical waves is a broader category that includes both longitudinal and transverse waves, as well as surface waves and torsional waves. It refers to waves that require a physical medium to propagate.
Electromagnetic waves are waves that can propagate through a vacuum and do not require a physical medium. They include light, radio waves, X-rays, and gamma rays, and are transverse waves.
